title: Indent in Insert Mode
subtitle: {key:Ctrl-T} and {key:Ctrl-D}, plus {key:0}{key:Ctrl-D} and {key:^}{key:Ctrl-D}.
summary: {key:Ctrl-T} indents, {key:Ctrl-D} outdents — both by one shiftwidth. The 0Ctrl-D variant kills all indent on the current line; ^{key:Ctrl-D} kills indent for this line only.

blocks.text: Indenting from inside *insert mode* keeps you in flow. {key:Ctrl-T} and {key:Ctrl-D} are the bread-and-butter; two special prefixes give finer control.

rows: Remove ALL indent on this line
Remove indent on this line only (restore on next)

blocks.title: Why Two Variants
blocks.text: Vim's autoindent often guesses the right indent for the next line. The {key:0}{key:Ctrl-D} variant says "strip indent from THIS line — typically used to flush a label/preprocessor line to column 0," while {key:^}{key:Ctrl-D} says "only this line; resume indent on the next." These are leftovers from the days of writing labels in C and assembly that *had* to be at column 0. They still work.
{key:Ctrl-T}/{key:Ctrl-D} respect `'shiftwidth'` (or `'tabstop'` if shiftwidth is 0). They're not the same as {key:Tab} — Tab inserts a literal tab character.
